markdown:
# USAR: federally funded magnet capacity with dated 2027 milestones, levered to humanoid unit volumes

## Bear case first
Every excerpt comes from a financing agreement, not from revenue. Disbursements depend on purchase commitments that may not convert to recognized sales [5], Magnet Project 2 still needs its power infrastructure plan resolved [1], and the government can claw back awards if the new projects do not commence by mid 2027 [19]. Nothing in the filings ties demand to humanoid robotics specifically; the magnets serve defense and EV demand first, and a humanoid delay to the 2030s leaves the thesis paying for capacity the theme never fills. Execution risk on four simultaneous plant builds is not addressed by the excerpts at all.

## Business today
The company is building domestic rare earth capacity across five awarded projects: the Round Top mine, two strip-cast neodymium-iron-boron metal plants, and two rare earth magnet plants [9] [18]. Commercial volume is still being contracted through pre-award and post-award purchase letters of intent rather than recognized sales [11] [12].

## Chain position
Enabler, rare earth magnet supply. High torque density joint motors depend on neodymium magnets, and a humanoid platform carries kilograms of magnet material across 30 to 40 joints. Magnet producers are paid per unit by every robot OEM regardless of which platform wins.

## Non-linear case
The company is adding magnet manufacturing capacity that does not exist in its current revenue base, backed by a federal Direct Funding Agreement [15] [8]. Disbursement milestones require minimum cumulative magnet purchase commitments, so contract wins are observable in filings as they happen [5] [6]. If humanoid deployment begins inside 2027 to 2030, it arrives as incremental demand on top of the defense and EV demand the funding already presumes, against capacity that is committed today [2] [19]. Success is a multiple of the current business because the current business is still pre-commercial.

## Filing evidence
- Three of five projects commenced before their clawback dates [2].
- Magnet Project 2 must commence by June 30, 2027 and Metal Project 2 by September 30, 2027 [19].
- Disbursements are gated on minimum cumulative Magnet Purchase Commitments [5], delivered to the Department within five business days of execution [6].
- Products are defined as rare-earth magnets and strip-cast neodymium-iron-boron metal, plus heavy rare earth oxides from Round Top [18].
- Magnet Project 2 is a new magnet manufacturing facility [15], one of two Additional Projects [8], with its power plan a disbursement condition [1].

## What would change our mind
- A missed commencement clawback date for either Additional Project, visible in an 8-K amendment to the funding agreement [19].
- Purchase commitment disclosures naming robotics customers, or failing to grow along the milestone schedule, visible in 8-K exhibits [5].
- A 10-K that shows magnet revenue concentrating entirely in defense programs, which would decouple the name from the humanoid theme.

# MATW: pass, the warehouse automation exposure was sold in December 2025

## Bear case first
This name reached the candidate list because its filings are dense with warehouse automation language, but the language is divestiture disclosure. The company sold its warehouse automation business to Duravant in December 2025 [4] [1], and every subsequent theme reference in the 10-Q is accounting for that sale: divestiture-related tax effects [2] [3], working capital effects [11], and prior-period sales prior to the divestiture [7]. There is no remaining theme exposure evidenced in the filings.

## Business today
Continuing operations center on memorialization products, product identification, and purpose-built engineered products including energy storage solutions for the electric vehicle market [6]. The automation, European packaging, and tooling businesses were all divested in the first quarter of fiscal 2026 [12].

## Chain position
Mapped to the second-order adopter node, warehouse and logistics automation, on keyword frequency. The read shows the mapping is a false positive: the company was a seller of automation capability, and it has now exited that position [1] [4].

## Non-linear case
None supported by the evidence. The divestiture removes the segment through which humanoid adoption could have inflected results [10]. Any future theme exposure would require redeploying divestiture proceeds into automation-adjacent capacity, which the excerpts do not evidence.

## Filing evidence
- The warehouse automation business was sold for cash consideration on December 31, 2025 [1], to Duravant LLC [4].
- The segment historically provided order fulfillment systems for picking and conveying products [0].
- Fiscal 2026 results reflect the divestiture in sales [6], gross profit [8], segment EBITDA [10], taxes [2] [3], and working capital [11].

## What would change our mind
- An 8-K disclosing an acquisition or investment that rebuilds automation exposure.
- A 10-K segment discussion showing engineered products revenue tied to robot component demand, which the current excerpts do not show.

Verdict: remove from the candidate list for this theme. The engine surfaced it correctly by keyword and the filing read correctly rejects it.

# KELYB: disrupted-side watch item, evidence read is too thin to act on

## Bear case first
The bear case here runs against the read itself, not the company. The excerpts are two sentences of earnings release boilerplate [0] [1]; they say nothing about automation, humanoids, or the light industrial mix that the disruption thesis targets. Scoring a staffing incumbent as disrupted on this evidence would be manufacturing a conclusion. The honest statement is that the filing read did not reach the documents where this risk would be discussed, which are the 10-K risk factors and segment disclosures.

## Business today
A staffing company that describes itself as having invented the staffing industry in 1946 and serving many industries and markets [0]. Nothing further is evidenced in the excerpts.

## Chain position
Disrupted, light industrial staffing. The node logic: staffing firms earn a spread on placed hours in exactly the repetitive warehouse and line tasks humanoids target first, so substitution shrinks the billable base directly. The logic is untested against this company's own disclosures; whether light industrial placements are a large share of revenue is an open question.

## Non-linear case
Not applicable on the long side. On the disrupted side, the non-linear risk is that per-hour billing has no floor under substitution: if humanoid labor becomes cheaper per task-hour, the affected billable base reprices toward it. Whether that base is 10 percent or 60 percent of revenue is not answerable from the excerpts and is the first question for a follow-up read.

## Filing evidence
- Company self-description and industry framing [0].
- Management uses staffing industry comparison measures in its reporting [1].

## What would change our mind
- A 10-K risk factor addition naming robotics or automation as a substitution risk to light industrial placements, which would confirm management sees the exposure.
- Segment disclosure quantifying light industrial revenue share, which would size the disrupted base.
- An 8-K announcing an automation partnership or staffing-for-robotics offering, which would move the name from disrupted toward second-order adopter.

Verdict: keep on the disrupted watch list. Rerun the read against the latest 10-K risk factors before drawing any conclusion.
